title: Chapter 19: The Price of Freedom
content: Elena's office had become their war room.

Mia was at the conference table, three monitors running, a fourth screen showing a live feed of the state court calendar. Legal pad pages were taped to the walls — timelines, organizational charts, financial flow diagrams. A whiteboard in the corner read: *SERENITY PEAK REPURPOSING PROJECT — PHASE 1: ACQUISITION*.
"The parent company filed for bankruptcy this morning," Mia announced as Chloe walked in. "Serenity Peak Holdings LLC. They're liquidating all assets."
"Good news or bad news?"
"Both. Good — the facility is officially for sale. Bad — there's already a buyer."
Chloe stopped. "Who?"
"A shell company. Registered in Delaware three days ago. The incorporator is listed as 'Chen Holdings LLC.'"
"Chen Holdings. My father's firm."
"Or someone on his behalf. The timing is too convenient — they filed the incorporation papers the same day the judge ordered the patient release. They knew the bankruptcy was coming."

Elena set down her coffee. "They're trying to buy the facility back before anyone else can. If they succeed, they control the narrative. They can 'reopen' with a new name, new branding, and no paper trail connecting them to the old operation."
"We can't let that happen," Chloe said.
"We won't," Mia said. "But we need a bid. And bids cost money."
"How much?"
Mia pulled up a number on her screen. "The court-appointed receiver has set the minimum bid at eight hundred thousand dollars."
Chloe stared at the number.
Eight hundred thousand dollars.
She had three hundred and forty-two dollars in her bank account.
"Okay," she said. "We need money."
"A lot of it," Mia clarified.
"I know what 'eight hundred thousand' means."
"Do you have any?"
"I have three hundred and forty-two dollars."
Mia blinked. "That's not going to work."
"I know."
Elena cleared her throat. "There are options. Legal options. We could start a GoFundMe. The story has national attention — people would donate."
"That could raise tens of thousands," Mia said. "Not eight hundred thousand."
"We could approach impact investors. Chloe's story is compelling. There are funds that invest in women-led social enterprises."
"That takes months. The bankruptcy sale happens in two weeks."
Silence.
"There's another option," Chloe said slowly.
They looked at her.
"My father's firm has assets frozen by the court. The same assets that funded Serenity Peak. If we can prove those assets were acquired through fraud, the court could release them to victims."
"That's a long legal process," Elena said. "Years, not weeks."

"Unless we find the evidence during the bankruptcy proceedings. If we can show that the shell company bidding on the facility is using money from the same fraudulent operation, the court could disqualify the bid and give us time to raise our own."
Mia's eyes lit up. "That's actually smart."
"I have smart moments."
"Rare ones."
"Shut up."
Elena smiled despite herself. "This could work. Let me make some calls."
She stepped out of the room. Chloe and Mia looked at each other across the table.
"We actually need to raise eight hundred thousand dollars in two weeks," Mia said.
"I know."
"That's insane."
"I know."
"I like it."
"I know you do."
The door opened. Tiffany walked in.

She looked different than she had at the facility. Her clothes were her own — a simple sweater and jeans, no designer labels. Her face was fuller, the hollows gone from her cheeks. She was eating again.
"I heard you were meeting," she said. "Amara's on her way."
"Good. Sit down."
Tiffany took a seat. She looked at the whiteboard, at the monitors, at the legal pads covering the walls.
"You're really doing this," she said.
"We're trying."
"How much do you need?"
Mia told her.
Tiffany did not blink. "I can get you half of that."
Chloe turned to look at her. "What?"
"I have a trust fund. My grandparents set it up when I was born. My parents control it, but I'm about to have a very serious conversation with them about what they owe me."
"Tiffany, that's — "
"My money. My choice. I spent twenty-two years letting other people decide what my life was worth. I'm done with that."
Chloe looked at her. The woman who had once apologized for taking up space was now offering four hundred thousand dollars to buy the prison that had held her.
"That's four hundred thousand," Chloe said. "We still need the other half."
"I can help with that."
Amara stepped through the door, phone in one hand, laptop in the other. She looked like she had been running — her hair was loose, her cheeks flushed.
"What did you do?" Mia asked.
"I just sold a start-up."
Silence.
"You what?" Chloe said.
"My father's company owned shares in a tech start-up he invested in five years ago. I called the CEO this morning and offered to sell him my father's stake at a discount. He said yes."
"You sold your father's shares without telling him?"
"I sold my inheritance early. My father can be angry about it later. Right now, I have two hundred thousand dollars."
Chloe stared at her.
Tiffany grinned. "Six hundred thousand. We're getting close."
"We still need two hundred," Mia said.
"I have an idea about that," Chloe said slowly.
They all looked at her.
"The GoFundMe. If the story stays in the news for another week, we could raise the rest. And I know exactly how to keep it in the news."
"How?"
Chloe pulled out her phone. "Dr. Webber's medical license hearing is tomorrow. I'm going to testify."
"You think that will raise money?" Tiffany asked.
"I think the news cameras will be there. I think I'll tell the truth. And I think the internet will do the rest."
Mia nodded slowly. "A viral moment. Press coverage of the hearing, linked to the GoFundMe. It could work."
"It will work," Chloe said.
She looked at the whiteboard. *SERENITY PEAK REPURPOSING PROJECT — PHASE 1: ACQUISITION.*
Six hundred thousand dollars raised. Two hundred thousand to go. A hearing tomorrow. A deadline in two weeks.
"We have a plan," she said. "Let's execute it."
That night, Chloe sat alone in Elena's guest room, staring at the ceiling.
The plan was solid. The funding was coming together. The hearing was tomorrow.
But sleep would not come.

She picked up her phone. Opened the news. The Serenity Peak story was still trending — #SerenityPeakScandal was the third trending topic on social media. Survivor stories were being shared by thousands of women. Donations were trickling into the GoFundMe that Elena had set up that afternoon.
Sixty-seven thousand dollars in six hours.
*Not bad,* she thought. *At this rate, we'll hit two hundred thousand in two days.*
She put the phone down and looked at the window. The city lights blurred through the glass.
Tomorrow, she would stand in front of a medical board and tell the world exactly what Dr. Webber had done. Not just to her, but to every woman who had been locked in his facility.
And then the cameras would roll. And the donations would flood in. And Serenity Peak would become theirs.
She closed her eyes and let herself feel it.
For the first time in years, she was looking forward to tomorrow.
